revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=ASCII Gamecube Keyboard Controller | Guid=3000-62 | Deck=A unique GameCube controller that was stretched to contain a full keyboard in the center. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/5/59593/2604995-2857285431-51UH5.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2604995-2857285431-51UH5.jpg","path":"5\/59593\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_tiny,square_medium,scale_small,square_small,scale_tiny,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,ignore_jpg_scale_medium,scale_super,screen_kubrick,ignore_jpg_scale_small","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> Released exclusively in Japan, this wired [https://www.giantbomb.com/ascii-corporation/3010-805 ASCII] controller was designed for use exclusively with [[Games/Phantasy_Star_Online_Episode_I_And_II|Phantasy Star Online Episode 1 andamp;amp;amp; 2]]. The keys allow include both Latin and Japanese characters, as well as all of the standard GameCube keys. Later, an identical keyboard was produced by [https://www.giantbomb.com/sammy-corporation/3010-569 Sammy] for the release of Phantasy Star Online Episode 3.

{{Accessory | Name=Access controller | Guid=3000-194 | Deck=A modular controller for the PS5 designed to help players with disabilities. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/33/338034/3469355-2310368420-de863.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3469355-2310368420-de863.jpg","path":"33\/338034\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,square_small,scale_super,scale_small,scale_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A modular controller for the PS5 designed to help players with disabilities.

{{Accessory | Name=Action Replay MAX | Guid=3000-105 | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/11/118833/3021092-max1.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3021092-max1.jpg","path":"11\/118833\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_medium,scale_medium,scale_super,scale_small,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,square_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,scale_tiny","caption":null},"background":[]}' />

{{Accessory | Name=AirStrike | Guid=3000-107 | Deck=The AirStrike drum controller was packed in with Power Gig: Rise of the Six string, a rhythm game for Xbox 360 and PlayStation 3. Rather than having physical pads to drum on, there were four colored sensors that detected when the included drum sticks entered the air space above them. Also included was a bass-drum pedal. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/12/120008/3024196-81deom0fa0l.ac_sl1500_.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3024196-81deom0fa0l.ac_sl1500_.jpg","path":"12\/120008\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,square_small,scale_super,scale_medium,square_tiny,scale_small,square_mini,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The AirStrike drum controller was packed in with Power Gig: Rise of the Six string, a rhythm game for Xbox 360 and PlayStation 3. Rather than having physical pads to drum on, there were four colored sensors that detected when the included drum sticks entered the air space above them. Also included was a bass-drum pedal.

{{Accessory | Name=Amazon Fire Game Controller | Guid=3000-73 | Deck=Wireless blue-tooth controller usable with the Amazon Fire TV. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/18/185687/2623552-amazon_firetv_controller.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2623552-amazon_firetv_controller.jpg","path":"18\/185687\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_tiny,square_mini,scale_small,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> Wireless blue-tooth controller usable with the Amazon Fire TV.

{{Accessory | Name=Apple Vision Pro | Guid=3000-198 | Deck=Apple Vision Pro is a upcoming mixed and virtual reality headset developed by Apple Inc. It was announced on June 5, 2023 at the Apple WWDC and is scheduled to go on sale in early 2024 in the US and later that year internationally. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/33/338034/3503203-4800760921-Apple.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3503203-4800760921-Apple.jpg","path":"33\/338034\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,square_medium,scale_small,scale_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,square_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Apple Vision Pro is a upcoming mixed and virtual reality headset developed by Apple Inc. It was announced on June 5, 2023 at the Apple WWDC and is scheduled to go on sale in early 2024 in the US and later that year internationally.

{{Accessory | Name=Arkanoid Contoller | Guid=3000-75 | Deck=An NES paddle controller packaged with Arkanoid. Consisted simply of a button, a spinner and an adjustment screw. Also worked with Arkanoid: Revenge of Doh and Super Chase H.Q. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/12/120008/2697699-0613590294-publi.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2697699-0613590294-publi.jpg","path":"12\/120008\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_super,square_tiny,square_medium,square_mini,scale_small,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> An NES paddle controller packaged with Arkanoid. Consisted simply of a button, a spinner and an adjustment screw. Also worked with Arkanoid: Revenge of Doh and Super Chase H.Q.

{{Accessory | Name=Asciiware Ascii Grip | Guid=3000-126 | Deck=A third-party controller for the Sony PlayStation that allows players to play games one-handed. Primarily used for role-playing games. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395542-screenshot2022-07-31at1.47.48pm.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395542-screenshot2022-07-31at1.47.48pm.png","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,scale_medium,scale_small,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_avatar,square_mini,scale_large,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> A third-party controller for the Sony PlayStation that allows players to play games one-handed. Primarily used for role-playing games.

{{Accessory | Name=Asciiware Ascii Pad PSS | Guid=3000-125 | Deck=A third-party controller for the Sony PlayStation. It features turbo and automatic switches for all four face buttons and all four shoulder buttons. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395532-6001975018-s-l16.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395532-6001975018-s-l16.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,scale_small,scale_medium,square_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_mini,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar,square_tiny","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A third-party controller for the Sony PlayStation. It features turbo and automatic switches for all four face buttons and all four shoulder buttons.

{{Accessory | Name=Asciiware Super Advantage | Guid=3000-122 | Deck=A third-party arcade stick for the Super Nintendo. It features turbo and automatic switches (including speed settings) for all four face buttons and both shoulder buttons. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/1427010-acc_advantage.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"1427010-acc_advantage.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,square_small,screen_medium,square_avatar,square_tiny,scale_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,scale_small,screen_kubrick","caption":"SNES Advantage"},"background":[]}' /> A third-party arcade stick for the Super Nintendo. It features turbo and automatic switches (including speed settings) for all four face buttons and both shoulder buttons.

{{Accessory | Name=Asciiware asciiPad | Guid=3000-121 | Deck=A third-party controller for the SNES that featured turbo and automatic switches for each of the face and shoulder buttons. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/1427011-acc_ascii.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"1427011-acc_ascii.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,square_small,screen_medium,square_avatar,square_tiny,scale_tiny,scale_small,ignore_jpg_scale_small,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick","caption":"SNES Asciipad"},"background":[]}' /> A third-party controller for the SNES that featured turbo and automatic switches for each of the face and shoulder buttons.

{{Accessory | Name=Atari 5200 Trakball Controller | Guid=3000-63 | Deck=Official Track Ball controller for the Atari 5200. Not all Atari 5200 games work with the controller however. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/13/132099/2531873-5200trak.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2531873-5200trak.jpg","path":"13\/132099\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_small,square_mini,square_tiny,square_medium,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_tiny,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> Official Track Ball controller for the Atari 5200. Not all Atari 5200 games work with the controller however.

{{Accessory | Name=Atari Driving Controller | Guid=3000-89 | Deck=Similar to the Atari paddle, only Indy 500 officially supports this controller | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/18/185326/2781495-1303797811-atari.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2781495-1303797811-atari.jpg","path":"18\/185326\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_medium,scale_small,scale_super,scale_tiny,square_tiny,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Atari driving controller is the pack-in controller for [[Games/Indy_500|Indy 500]]. Though similar in appearance to the [[Accessories/Atari_Paddle_Controllers|paddle controller]], the driving controller's spinner can be rotated infinitely (the paddle has a hard lock after a complete spin, and rebounds to center). The driving controller is only supported by the single game, but like the paddle, underwent a minor re-design in 1978, a year after release. The driving controller was packed in pairs with Indy 500, but each controller has its own input, allowing only two-player simultaneous play (compared to the paddles' four). Though the controller is generically branded for driving games, other driving games (such as [[Games/Night_Driver|Night Driver]]) use the paddle or [[Objects/Atari_CX40_Joystick|joystick]], not the driving controller.

{{Accessory | Name=Atari Paddle Controllers | Guid=3000-88 | Deck=Spinner controllers packed in with the Atari VCS/2600 game console | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/18/185326/2781494-1489126706-800px.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2781494-1489126706-800px.jpg","path":"18\/185326\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,scale_super,square_mini,square_small,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_tiny,square_avatar,scale_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Atari paddle controllers are analog spinner game controllers for the [[Platforms/Atari_2600|Atari VCS/2600]] console. Paddles were manufactured in pairs, and two controllers plug into a single input, resulting in up to four-player multiplayer (with two paddle pairs). Additionally, each controller has a side-mounted button. While the original version shipped in 1977 with the Atari VCS, an updated version was released in 1978 with minor differences in the sticker graphics, and is somewhat shorter overall. Over 50 games were designed to use the paddle controller over the life of the system. Like the [[Objects/Atari_CX40_Joystick|Atari joystick]], paddles are compatible with certain other consoles which use the same input scheme. One notable example is [[Games/Super_Breakout|Super Breakout]] for the [[Platforms/Atari_8_bit|Atari 800]], which has 4 inputs, allowing up to 8-player simultaneous play. The similar Atari [[Accessories/Atari_Driving_Controller|driving controller]] was released in 1977, but is officially only supported by [[Games/Indy_500|Indy 500]].

{{Accessory | Name=Atari Track & Field Controller | Guid=3000-93 | Deck=Included with the Atari 2600 Track & Field port | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/18/185326/2782174-9337905882-con_A.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2782174-9337905882-con_A.jpg","path":"18\/185326\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,scale_super,square_medium,square_tiny,square_small,square_avatar,scale_avatar,square_mini,scale_tiny,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Atari 2600 release of [[Games/Track_And_Field|Track andamp;amp;amp; Field]] included this controller to mimic the Arcade's button mashing input. The controller buttons are simply mapped to the joystick "left", "right", and "fire". The controller was also sold separately for use on systems with compatible inputs.

{{Accessory | Name=ComLynx Cable | Guid=3000-57 | Deck=Accessory cable allowing connected Lynx gameplay. }}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":[],"background":[]}' /> This official Atari Lynx accessory allows up to 18 Lynxes to be connected for simultaneous multiplayer gameplay. 36 games support the ComLynx cable, usually supporting 2-4 units' connections. Each player must have a physical copy of the game.

{{Accessory | Name=DJ Hero Turntable Controller | Guid=3000-156 | Deck=Released in 2009 by Activision, it is a unique controller designed specifically for use with the DJ Hero series of games. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396241-1866646529-djher.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396241-1866646529-djher.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_medium,square_medium,scale_small,square_tiny,scale_super,square_mini,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Released in 2009 by Activision, it is a unique controller designed specifically for use with the DJ Hero series of games.

{{Accessory | Name=Dreamcast Controller | Guid=3000-136 | Deck=The official controller designed by Sega for the Dreamcast. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395670-9948956626-AEY18.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395670-9948956626-AEY18.png","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_small,square_small,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_super,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official controller designed by Sega for the Dreamcast.

{{Accessory | Name=Dreamcast Keyboard | Guid=3000-46 | Deck=A keyboard accessory for the Dreamcast. Supported by the web browser and a handful of games. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/1426196-acc_keyboard.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"1426196-acc_keyboard.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,square_small,screen_medium,square_avatar,square_avatar,scale_tiny,scale_small,square_tiny,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> A keyboard accessory for the Dreamcast. Supported by the web browser and a handful of games.

{{Accessory | Name=Dreamcast Maracas | Guid=3000-14 | Deck=Shake your boot-ay! | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/24/10983-samba.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"10983-samba.jpg","path":"0\/24\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,square_small,screen_medium,square_avatar,scale_small,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' />
==Overview== Released specifically for the [[Companies/Sega|Sega]] [[Platforms/Dreamcast|Dreamcast]] title [[Games/Samba_De_Amigo|Samba de Amigo]], the maracas controller was a unique and unorthodox method of controlling a video game for the time.
==Use== Samba de Amigo is a rhythm game focusing on the use shaking of the maracas in time to the music by following the onscreen graphics. The game has players shake the maracas over six positions (3 per maraca) at high, medium, and low altitudes, timed precisely to "hit" graphics that radiate out from a central point to the various locations the maracas must be shook. Additionally, the game requires players to produce humorous poses, by moving the maracas to any two of the six positions and holding them there. While primarily designed for use with Samba de Amigo, the maracas also work with two other games, [[Companies/Bandai_Namco_Entertainment|Namco]]'s [[Games/Mr_Driller|Mr. Driller]] and [[Companies/SNK|SNK]]'s [[Games/Cool_Cool_Toon|Cool Cool Toon]].

{{Accessory | Name=Dreamcast Mouse | Guid=3000-45 | Deck=Mouse accessory for the Dreamcast. Supported by the web browser and a handful of games. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/13/131447/2446277-7856376064-688px.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2446277-7856376064-688px.jpg","path":"13\/131447\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_small,square_tiny,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_medium,scale_super,scale_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> Mouse accessory for the Dreamcast. Supported by the web browser and a handful of games.

revision.comment: Fix duplicated lead from prior repair edit (guid-audit)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Dreamcast Twin Stick | Guid=3000-100 | Deck=A Dreamcast peripheral controller designed for use with Cyber Troopers Virtual-On: Oratorio Tangram. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/23/232057/2913426-642px-dreamcasttwinstick.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2913426-642px-dreamcasttwinstick.jpg","path":"23\/232057\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_mini,square_medium,square_small,scale_small,scale_medium,scale_super,square_tiny,scale_tiny,scale_large,square_avatar,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' />
Released 12/09/1999 only in Japan, the Dreamcast Twinstick is a 4 button, 2 trigger, dual stick controller. The controller was made specifically for use with the dreamcast release of Virtual-On: Oratorio Tangram.
The dimensions of the controller are: 2.25" tall, 11" wide, and 8" deep. There is a slight slant to the top of the controller starting at 1.75", from the player facing side of controller, to the top of the controller. While the top of the controller is plastic the bottom of the controller is metal with 6 black foam circular pads to prevent sliding. There are 18 screw holes on the controller and 16 screws (two screws go through both 2 holes each) holding it together, 6 screw holes on the right stick, 6 on the left stick, and six on the bottom of the controller.
The controller has 1 VMU port at the top of the controller, below this is a 75" wire allowing the console to connect to the dreamcast controller port. Each joystick on the controller has one button on top to be pressed by the player's thumb and one on the front to be pressed by the player's index finger.
The only model number sold of this controller is: HKT-7500
==Compatible Games== * Virtual-On: Oratorio Tangram * OutTrigger * Quake III Arena * Rez * Ikaruga * Soulcalibur * Virtua Tennis

{{Accessory | Name=Dreamcast VMU | Guid=3000-137 | Deck=The Visual Memory Unit (VMU) is the official storage device designed by Sega for the Dreamcast. It features an LCD screen and buttons, which some games utilize as a mini portable gaming system. The screen is also visible while plugged into the controller. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395695-5842407154-Sega-.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395695-5842407154-Sega-.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_small,square_medium,square_mini,scale_super,scale_medium,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Visual Memory Unit (VMU) is the official storage device designed by Sega for the Dreamcast. It features an LCD screen and buttons, which some games utilize as a mini portable gaming system. The screen is also visible while plugged into the controller.

{{Accessory | Name=DualSense Charging Station | Guid=3000-188 | Deck=The official controller charging product designed by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation 5. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396799-0847620025-8340377_R_SET.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396799-0847620025-8340377_R_SET.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_medium,scale_medium,scale_super,square_small,scale_tiny,square_tiny,square_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_mini","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official controller charging product designed by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation 5.

{{Accessory | Name=DualSense Controller | Guid=3000-113 | Deck=The DualSense controller is the primary controller for the PlayStation 5. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3180576-49747503557_566da5ebc8_3k.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3180576-49747503557_566da5ebc8_3k.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,scale_super,square_small,square_tiny,scale_medium,scale_small,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar,scale_tiny","caption":""},"background":{"file":"3180577-49746632758_470c1cca29_3k.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_medium,screen_kubrick,square_tiny,scale_medium,square_small,screen_kubrick_wide,scale_tiny,scale_large,square_mini,scale_small,scale_avatar,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":""}}' /> The DualSense controller is the primary controller for the PlayStation 5.

{{Accessory | Name=DualShock 3 | Guid=3000-29 | Deck=The DualShock 3 is an updated wireless PlayStation 3 controller that incorporates rumble motors as well as motion-sensing SIXAXIS support. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/495/907335-dualshock3_screen_01_big.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"907335-dualshock3_screen_01_big.jpg","path":"0\/495\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,square_small,screen_medium,square_avatar,scale_small,square_tiny,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":"DualShock 3 Front View"},"background":[]}' />
==Overview== The controller is a combination of technologies found in the [[Accessories/DualShock_2|DualShock 2]]and the [[Accessories/SIXAXIS_Controller|SIXAXIS]]controllers. It has the trigger-like L2 and R2 button modifications and motion sensors found on the SIXAXIS, and the rumble technologies similar to the DualShock 2's. It was unable to be released until over a year into the [[Platforms/PlayStation_3|PlayStation 3]]'s lifespan due to a lawsuit between the creators of the [[Concepts/Contextual_Controller_Rumble|rumble]]technology, Immersion, and Sony over their use of rumble in their previous controllers. After an out-of-court settlement, the DualShock 3 was announced and subsequently released. The DualShock 3 is also heavier, due to the new motors, which addresses a complaint of many about the SIXAXIS being too light.
The DualShock 3 is available in a number of different colors; black, blue, red, pink, silver, white, jungle green, and a baseball stitching pattern.
To see a list of PlayStation 3 games that support the SIXAXIS tilt functionality, see the [[Concepts/SIXAXIS_Support|SIXAXIS Support]]concept page.
==Input== * 2 analog sticks * Directional D-pad (pressure sensitive) * 3 digital buttons * 8 pressure sensitive buttons * 2 analog triggers * Motion sensing around six axes
==Output== The DualShock 3 controller is capable of communicating with a PS3 console via a tethered USB 2.0 connection, or wirelessly using Bluetooth.
==Color Variations==
====Standard Colors==== Standard colors can be found bundled with standard consoles or for sale on their own. * Charcoal Black * Ceramic White * Deep Red * Metallic Blue * Metallic Gold * Metallic Grey * Satin Silver - lighter-colored, less texture than Metallic Grey * Urban Camouflage * Jungle Green - olive-green front shell, black back shell * Candy Pink * Candy Blue
====Game-specific Variants==== Game-specific variants can be found bundled with game-themed consoles, bundled with the games themselves, or (rarely) for sale on their own * Gunmetal Grey Bundled with the MGS4 edition PS3 console. Available in Japan and Western markets. Deep gray-olive, distinct from other grey/silver controllers. * Uncharted 3 Special Bundled with Uncharted 3 in Japan. Yellow/Brown color scheme with printed patterns. * Final Fantasy XIII Lightning Edition Bundled with Final Fantasy XIII in Japan. White controller with pink Lightning-themed printed pattern. * Lightning Returns Special Bundled with Lightning Returns: Final Fantasy XIII in Japan. Sold on its own elsewhere. Black controller with printed patterns. * MLB 11 The Show Edition Bundled with MLB 11 The Show in Western markets. White controller with 'baseball' printed patterns. * Tales Of Xillia 2 Special Bundled with Tales Of Xillia 2 in Japan. Black controller with printed patterns. * God Of War Ascension Special Bundled with God Of War Ascension in Japan and Western markets, available on its own in European markets. Yellow controller with printed patterns.
====Translucent Variants==== Translucent variants feature tinted see-through plastic, exposing the internals. * Crimson Red Available in most markets. Target-exclusive in the US. * Cosmic Blue Available in most markets. Bundled with Infamous 2 in EU markets. * Slate Grey Available in most markets. * Crystal / Clear Japan-only.
==Firmware Requirements== PlayStation 3: version 1.94 or higher PlayStation or PlayStation 2: version 2.00 or higher

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-42)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=DualShock 4 | Guid=3000-42 | Deck=The fourth iteration of the DualShock controller for PlayStation 4. It includes a touch pad, light bar, and Share button. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/2440235-ps4dual.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2440235-ps4dual.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_small,square_avatar,scale_tiny,square_mini,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,ignore_jpg_scale_medium,scale_kubrick,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":{"file":"2440233-ps4ds4.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_kubrick,scale_tiny,scale_medium,screen_kubrick_wide,ignore_jpg_scale_medium,scale_super,scale_large,scale_small,scale_avatar,square_mini,screen_kubrick","caption":""}}' />
==Overview== The DualShock 4 controller is Sony's first significant redesign since the [[Accessories/DualShock_Analog_Controller|DualShock]]launched in 1997 as an updated controller for the original PlayStation. Sticking with the overall basic form factor, the DualShock 4 is a slightly larger, more ergonomic iteration on its predecessor. New features include a textured underside to improve grip, reshaped L2 and R2 buttons that better resemble analog triggers, reshaped left and right analog sticks that are concave on the top, a capacitive touch pad located where the start and select buttons were in previous versions, an built-in mono speaker, a headset jack, and a Move-styled, multi-color light bar. It has a built-in 3.65V Lithium-ion rechargeable battery with 1000mAh capacity.
==Input== * Two analog sticks (Improved fidelity and materials over the DualShock 3) * Pressure sensitive, four button D-pad * Traditional Δ, ☐, O, X face buttons * Two analog shoulder buttons (L1, R1) * Two analog triggers (L2, R2) * Face mounted 3-point clickable capacitive touch pad * Two digital face buttons (Share, Options) * Six-axis motion sensing (newly built high precision sensors) * Headset jack (mono headset will be bundled with PS4)
==Output== The DualShock 4 has an in-built mono speaker "to deliver high-fidelity sound effects" from the controller and a 3 color LED light bar similar to the orb found in the [[Accessories/PlayStation_Move|PlayStation Move]].
==LED Light Bar==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/1992/2440229-ps4_controller_move_light_bar.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/1992/2440229-ps4_controller_move_light_bar.jpg|align=right|width=320|caption=Dualshock 4 Light Bar - Colored Blue}} The top mounted color light bar on the DualShock 4 can identify different controllers during local multiplayer sessions and change color dynamically to provide useful information to players, such as glowing red to indicate they've taken damage.
The DualShock 4 also works alongside the [http://www.giantbomb.com/playstation-4-eye/3000-43 PlayStation Camera], another peripheral which can "sense the depth of the environment in front of it and also track the 3D location of the controller via its light bar."
==Connectivity== The DualShock 4 utilizes Bluetooth 2.1+EDR to communicate with the PlayStation 4. It also has a Micro-USB (Micro-B) port to communicate and charge.
==Compatibility== Aside from being the primary interface device for the Playstation 4, the DualShock 4 is additionally compatible with the Playstation 3, Windows PCs, and Mac OSX. The compatibility is limited to basic plug-and-play functions as a generic wired controller via a standard Micro-USB cable or via bluetooth. The touch pad, motion sensors, and built-in speaker functions are not currently accessible.
Additional compatibility can be added to the device on Windows PCs by utilizing XInput mappers such as [http://dsdcs.com/DS4Tool DS4Windows] which make the controller compatible with games that can only utilize the XInput standard as well as add additional functionality such as touchpad to mouse support and various lightbar configuration options.

{{Accessory | Name=DualShock 4 Back Button Attachment | Guid=3000-112 | Deck=An attachment for the Dualshock 4 controller that adds two programmable back buttons. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/15/154673/3150956-0130580971-duals.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3150956-0130580971-duals.jpg","path":"15\/154673\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,scale_medium,scale_small,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_tiny,square_mini,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> An attachment for the Dualshock 4 controller that adds two programmable back buttons.

{{Accessory | Name=EyeToy | Guid=3000-145 | Deck=Released in 2003, the EyeToy is a camera designed by Richard Marks for the PlayStation 2 that is capable of tracking players' body movements and offering mixed reality gaming. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396023-2732416267-ps2-e.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396023-2732416267-ps2-e.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_medium,scale_small,scale_super,square_medium,square_tiny,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_mini,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Released in 2003, the EyeToy is a camera designed by Richard Marks for the PlayStation 2 that is capable of tracking players' body movements and offering mixed reality gaming.

{{Accessory | Name=FlexStrike Wireless Fight Stick | Guid=3000-205 | Deck=Originally codenamed as "Project Defiant", Sony's first wireless fight stick is set to release in 2026. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/33/338034/3752371-8920851251-54568.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3752371-8920851251-54568.jpg","path":"33\/338034\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_medium,square_medium,scale_super,scale_small,square_tiny,square_avatar,screen_medium,scale_large,square_mini,screen_kubrick,scale_avatar,scale_tiny","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Originally codenamed as "Project Defiant", Sony's first wireless fight stick is set to release in 2026.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-47)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=GameCube Controller | Guid=3000-47 | Deck=The official controller designed by Nintendo for the GameCube. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/13/131447/2446279-1681183791-tumbl.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2446279-1681183791-tumbl.jpg","path":"13\/131447\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_small,square_tiny,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_medium,scale_super,scale_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' />
==Overview==
{{GBFigure|src=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_medium/19/194918/2653289-450px-gccontroller_layout.svg.png|full=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/19/194918/2653289-450px-gccontroller_layout.svg.png|align=right|width=450}} The GameCube Controller (DOL-003) was released alongside the Nintendo GameCube on September 14th 2001 in Japan. The controller was included with the console and was also available separately.
The controller included 6 digital buttons - A, B, X, Y and Z alongside a start button - 2 joysticks, a directional pad, and two analogue triggers with digital button capability.
Haptic Feedback was present in the controller thanks to the inclusion of a rumble motor.
==Variants== The GameCube controller was available in a variety of colours - the standard colours were "Indigo"(Purple), "Jet Black", "Platinum" (Silver) and "Spice" (Orange).
===<ins>[[Accessories/Wavebird|Wavebird Wireless Controller]]</ins>===
{{GBFigure|src=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/1992/1450767-wavebird6big.jpg|full=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/1992/1450767-wavebird6big.jpg|align=right|width=320}} A wireless version of the controller manufactured by Nintendo which was larger and included a battery compartment and on off switch. The Wavebird was available to purchase separately to the console at a slightly increased price. However, unlike the normal wired controller, the Wavebird did not support Haptic Feedback.
===<ins>LodgeNet Controller</ins>===
{{GBFigure|src=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/19/194918/2653296-lodgenet%20gamecube%20controller.jpg|full=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/19/194918/2653296-lodgenet%20gamecube%20controller.jpg|align=right|width=225}} A version of the controller which included extra buttons in order to navigate the menus of the LodgeNet service which was available at many North American Hotels and allowed customers to have pay-per-play access to GameCube titles.
===<ins>[[Accessories/ASCII_Gamecube_Keyboard_Controller|GameCube ASCII Keyboard Controller]]</ins>===
{{GBFigure|src=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/5/59593/2604995-2857285431-51UH5.jpg|full=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/5/59593/2604995-2857285431-51UH5.jpg|align=right|width=320}} Released only in Japan, this version of the controller was stretched to contain a full sized ASCII keyboard.
===<ins>Wii U Smash Bros. Controller</ins>===
{{GBFigure|src=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/14/142092/2674242-9993496050-WiiU_.jpg|full=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/14/142092/2674242-9993496050-WiiU_.jpg|align=right|width=320}} In 2014 Nintendo announced a re-release of the GameCube controller along with a 4-port usb adapter for the Wii U to coincide with the launch of [https://www.giantbomb.com/super-smash-bros/3030-35573 Super Smash Bros. for Wii U].
===<ins>Other Special Controllers</ins>=== Limited Edition controllers were released alongside games and through services such as Club Nintendo. These controllers featured unique colour schemes and/or logos. Some controllers were released alongside limited edition coloured GameCube systems. The Japan exclusive Panasonic-Q featured its own special grey coloured, Panasonic branded, controller.

{{Accessory | Name=GameCube Controller Adapter | Guid=3000-74 | Deck=The GameCube Controller Adapter is an official adapter for Wii U that connects to the console via USB to support the usage of GameCube Controllers on the console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/8/82063/2689468-04---wh1ay07.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2689468-04---wh1ay07.jpg","path":"8\/82063\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,scale_super,square_tiny,square_medium,square_small,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The device features four [[Accessories/GameCube_Controller|GameCube Controller]] ports on the front and is connected to the Wii or Wii U console with two USB cables. The black USB serves to power the adapter and transfer data, and the grey USB is used for rumble. [[Accessories/Wavebird|Wavebird]]controllers can be used with this adapter, too. The adapter is available bundled with Super Smash Bros. for Wii U both either with just the adapter and the game but also with a copy of the game, the adapter and a black Smash-branded GameCube Controller. The adapter can also be purchased separately for £17.99/€19.99/$19.99. An initial listing on the official Nintendo UK web-store mentioned that the device works on Wii and can be used for games supporting the Wii Classic Controller line and the Wii U Pro Controller but this seems to have been a mistake since it has been swiftly removed from the item description.

{{Accessory | Name=GameCube DK Bongos | Guid=3000-153 | Deck=Designed by Namco Bandai, the DK Bongos are an officially licensed controller for the Nintendo GameCube that are primarily used for the Donkey Konga series of rhythm games. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396223-8743112087-13125.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396223-8743112087-13125.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_medium,scale_super,scale_small,square_medium,square_mini,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Designed by Namco Bandai, the DK Bongos are an officially licensed controller for the Nintendo GameCube that are primarily used for the Donkey Konga series of rhythm games.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-149)

revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=GameCube Memory Card | Guid=3000-154 | Deck=The official storage device designed by Nintendo for the GameCube console. The original version (gray) had space for 59 blocks of storage. Two later versions included 251 blocks (black) and 1019 blocks (white). | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396230-0543261567-latest.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396230-0543261567-latest.png","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"scale_medium,square_small,scale_super,scale_small,square_mini,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,scale_large,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official storage device designed by Nintendo for the GameCube console. The original version (gray) had space for 59 blocks of storage. Two later versions included 251 blocks (black) and 1019 blocks (white).

revision.comment: Point cover at a rendition that actually serves

revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Game Boy Advance Link Cable | Guid=3000-54 | Deck=Connect two Game Boy Advances together for head-to-head action! | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/16/164924/2486361-gba_link_cable_en.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2486361-gba_link_cable_en.jpg","path":"16\/164924\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_small,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_tiny,square_tiny,square_medium,ignore_jpg_scale_medium,scale_super,screen_kubrick,ignore_jpg_scale_small","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> Connect two Game Boy Advances together for head-to-head action!

{{Accessory | Name=Game Boy Player | Guid=3000-81 | Deck=A hardware accessory that attached to the bottom of the GameCube through its high-speed parallel port. This allowed Game Boy, Game Boy Color, or Game Boy Advance games to be played on a TV with GameCube controllers or a GBA. Also required was the included Game Boy Player Startup disc that went in the GameCube's disc drive. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/3/35607/2743513-gamecube-game-boy-player.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2743513-gamecube-game-boy-player.jpg","path":"3\/35607\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_medium,scale_super,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_small,square_avatar,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_medium,scale_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A hardware accessory that attached to the bottom of the GameCube through its high-speed parallel port. This allowed Game Boy, Game Boy Color, or Game Boy Advance games to be played on a TV with GameCube controllers or a GBA. Also required was the included Game Boy Player Startup disc that went in the GameCube's disc drive.

{{Accessory | Name=Game Boy Printer | Guid=3000-55 | Deck=A device that was normally used in conjunction with the Game Boy Camera, but could also be used to print snapshots from within specific games, usually as stickers. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/16/164924/2486362-gbp.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2486362-gbp.jpg","path":"16\/164924\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_small,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> A device that was normally used in conjunction with the Game Boy Camera, but could also be used to print snapshots from within specific games, usually as stickers.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-21)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Game Genie | Guid=3000-21 | Deck=The Game Genie is an accessory for several platforms that facilitates the hacking and activation of otherwise inaccessible cheats and content. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/25/10908-GameGenies.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"10908-GameGenies.jpg","path":"0\/25\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,square_small,screen_medium,square_avatar,scale_small,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":"Game Genies for several platforms."},"background":[]}' />
==Overview== The Game Genie is line of cheat devices that was manufactured by Galoob. These devices can be connected to a game cartridge, and then inserted into a console, functioning essentially as physical extension of the cartridge. By using codes input into a Game Genie menu before booting the game proper, hex values in the game could be altered to add abilities or access content that game developers did not intend.
Due to their nature as unsupported ROM hack devices, Game Genie codes are not guaranteed to work. And because some games are released in multiple revisions with slight changes, a Game Genie code that may work in the original printing may not work in a later version.
Game Genies were released for the[[Platforms/Nintendo_Entertainment_System|NES]],[[Platforms/Super_Nintendo_Entertainment_System|SNES]],[[Platforms/Game_Boy|Game Boy]],[[Platforms/Genesis|Genesis]], and the[[Platforms/Game_Gear|Game Gear]]. The Game Genie was the predecessor to devices such as the [[Accessories/Pro_Action_Replay|Action Replay]], Code Breaker, and GameShark, which provided similar functionality for more modern systems such as the [[Platforms/Nintendo_64|Nintendo 64]], [[Platforms/PlayStation_2|PlayStation 2]], and [[Platforms/Xbox|Xbox]].
Hyperkin later acquired the Game Genie name and has since released a save editor compatible with the [[Platforms/PlayStation_3|PlayStation 3]] and a cheat device for the [[Platforms/Nintendo_DS|Nintendo DS]]. However, these products should not be confused in any way with the original line of Game Genie devices.
==NES==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/7465/875194-nes_game_genie.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/7465/875194-nes_game_genie.jpg|align=right|caption=NES Game Genie}} The Game Genie for the NES had a unique quirk -- since the games had to be pushed in and down into the console, the Game Genie had to be built with a system to override that feature, which made it so the Nintendo game would be jutting out the front of the console. This design put stress on the contact pins inside the NES and could lead to the pins becoming bent and twisted. The damage could potentially lead to the NES being unable to play games without the use of a Game Genie inserted.
==SNES==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/7465/875196-snesgamegenie_pal.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/7465/875196-snesgamegenie_pal.jpg|align=left|caption=SNES Game Genie}} On the SNES, the Game Genie worked well until the release of [[Companies/Nintendo|Nintendo]]'s Super FX Chip. Games featuring these chips had extra chips that plugged into the left and right sides of the main slot, though at the time of the release of the Game Genie, these slots were unused so there was no need to create chips to connect to them. Accordingly, the Game Genie was unable to access all the code necessary to add the cheats. There were some games that did work even without the extra plugs -- [[Games/Mega_Man_X2|Mega Man X2]] and [[Games/Mega_Man_X3|Mega Man X3]] both worked perfectly with the Game Genie.
==Game Boy==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/7465/875197-game_genie.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/7465/875197-game_genie.jpg|align=right|caption=Game Boy Game Genie}} The Game Boy version of the Game Genie was a cartridge similar to other Game Boy cartridges, except it had another slot on top to insert a regular Game Boy cartridge. This version of the Game Genie was also packaged with a small code booklet. When used in the [[Platforms/Game_Boy_Color|Game]] Boy Color, it had to be pushed down. This problem can be fixed by Using it in a GBA SP.
==Genesis==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/7465/875200-segengg.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/7465/875200-segengg.jpg|align=left|caption=Genesis Game Genie}} The Genesis/Mega Drive version of the Game Genie can also serve as a country converter. Most Genesis cartridges were only region-locked by the shape of the cartridge and a few bytes in the header of the ROM, and by using the Game Genie, the lock could be circumvented.
==Game Gear==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/7465/875201-gg_game_genie.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/7465/875201-gg_game_genie.jpg|align=right|caption=Game Gear Game Genie}} The Game Gear version of the Game Genie had quite an interesting design. Upon being pushed into the cartridge slot, another slot would open on the top of the Game Genie where players could place the regular Game Gear cartridge. This version of the Game Genie also came with a small code booklet.

{{Accessory | Name=Game Master | Guid=3000-102 | Deck=A cheat device for the MSX which allowed players to change variables in games that Konami published for the system. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/16/164924/2988127-1476187968-87535.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2988127-1476187968-87535.jpg","path":"16\/164924\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,scale_super,scale_small,square_mini,square_small,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_tiny,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A cheat device for the MSX which allowed players to change variables in games that Konami published for the system.

{{Accessory | Name=Guitar Hero Gibson SG Controller | Guid=3000-138 | Deck=The original Guitar Hero guitar designed by Red Octane. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395710-4769621026-gibso.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395710-4769621026-gibso.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,square_small,scale_super,scale_small,scale_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,scale_large,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The original Guitar Hero guitar designed by Red Octane.

page.title: Accessories/Guitar Hero Gibson X plorer Controller

revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Guitar Hero Gibson X-plorer Controller | Guid=3000-157 | Deck=Designed by Red Octane, the X-plorer guitar was originally packaged with the Xbox 360 version of Guitar Hero II. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396258-8090763843-latest.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396258-8090763843-latest.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_tiny,square_medium,square_small,scale_super,scale_medium,scale_small,square_mini,scale_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,scale_large,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Designed by Red Octane, the X-plorer guitar was originally packaged with the Xbox 360 version of Guitar Hero II.

revision.comment: Point cover at a rendition that actually serves
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=HTC Vive | Guid=3000-84 | Deck=The HTC Vive is a virtual reality headset for the PC, developed in co-production between Valve and HTC. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/14/141373/2760903-9891272678-HTC%252.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2760903-9891272678-HTC%2.jpg","path":"14\/141373\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,scale_super,square_tiny,square_small,square_medium,square_mini,square_avatar,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_medium,scale_tiny,ignore_jpg_scale_small,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":{"file":"2760905-5994778500-HTC%2.JPG","path":"14\/141373\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_tiny,screen_kubrick,screen_kubrick_wide,scale_medium,square_mini","caption":""}}' /> The consumer version of the Vive shipped out in June of 2016. The Vive supports 3 main types of experiences: [[Concepts/Seated_VR|Seated]], [[Concepts/Standing_VR|Standing]], and [[Concepts/Roomscale|Roomscale]]. [[Concepts/Seated_VR|Seated]]and [[Concepts/Standing_VR|Standing]]are as they sound and require little elaboration. [[Concepts/Roomscale|Roomscale]]requires a space of a minimum of 1m by 1m, extending up to 3m by 3m. The room needs to have the "VR Lighthouses" installed, and then to be configured by pacing it out with the controllers. A caveat of this is that mirrors will interfere with the invisible beams that are used for lay out the space, confusing the system. Before purchasing the Vive, running the Steam VR Performance test is recommended, to confirm that system will be able to handle the graphical requirements.
